The example is correct. From the initial URL

```python
f = furl('http://www.google.com/?one=1&two=2')
```

`three` is added with

```python
f.args['three'] = '3'
```

and `one` is removed with

```python
del f.args['one']
```

resulting in `http://www.google.com/?two=2&three=3`.

They had no idea nothing was going on "behind the scenes" and that an error occurred and no response was ever coming. So I figured some kind of timer would be appropriate. The 3 second number was sort of arbitrary. I can update the text to have some caveats to say it's not necessarily an error, and perhaps make it 10 seconds instead of 3.  It *could* be a configurable thing, but I think I'd rather hold off on adding configuration options for every little thing and try to get the behavior helpful in all situations, including #128 .

I agree large file sizes can cause gdbgui to freeze, and I don't think this is a rust-specific problem. I have been thinking about how to handle this and created issue #131. Take a look and add comments there if you're interested.

> rust gdb integration appears a little bit weird in 7.*, locals (or variables in mi) can contain duplicates so if you are in a function the function argument can be present with an arg value in its details but also again as a non function argument local. This breaks the Locals component in RightBar as it violates Reacts unique key constraint when iterating over sorted_local_objs as they have duplicate keys. Also children of a local can contain multiple annonymous values breaking the unique key constraint in GbdVariable when it iterates over children.

Good feedback, though I don't generally develop in rust. The (very simple) [example](https://github.com/cs01/gdbgui/blob/master/examples/rust/hello.rs) in gdbgui looks fine when I step through it. Perhaps you can post some example code that fails as you described.

> 这个我看代码没法先有太大区别，只是转档时存储的格式不一致，所以就用解析的结构套了一下，基本通用。如下图:
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15207429/36514629-4c215504-17b0-11e8-909e-50e28804b9e7.png)

2. 某天的某只股票第一笔数据是明文的，后面每分钟内的交易详情中，时间、价格、成交量都是通过两个hash表存储的相对价格，需要根据hash表转换后才可以使用。
> 这个已经逆完了，只是还没写程序验证。

由于今天开始上班了，我这边的时间也确实无法保证，为了不让大家白等。我将我这边逆出来的结构及相关代码传上来。谁要是有时间就一起写程序验证一下吧，有问题再及时沟通。

```
int __stdcall SetTradeTime(int argTickObj)
{
  int result; // eax@1

  result = 0x23Au;
  if ( (_WORD)argTickObj < 0 )
  {
    if ( (_WORD)argTickObj <= (signed __int16)0x78u )
      return result;                            // 如果时间小于0则是标记570分钟=9点30.
  } else {
    if ( (_WORD)argTickObj <= (signed __int16)0x78u )
      return argTickObj + 0x23A;                // 上午时间
  }

  if ( (_WORD)argTickObj <= (signed __int16)0xF0u )
    result = argTickObj + 0x294;                // 下午时间需要补齐中午的时间
  return result;
}
```

I've pushed a change that addresses the problem of file descriptor reuse.  It turns out that you can (apparently) easily solve this problem by putting a small wrapper object around the integer file descriptor and passing that into Curio instead.  Although the integer file descriptor value might be reused, the wrapper object is not.  So, it becomes possible to detect changes to the file via that route.   I also made the kernel NOT apply the debouncing optimization if bare integer file descriptors are used--an extra layer of safety perhaps.

If at all possible, I'd like to try and keep the so-called debouncing optimization.  Benchmarking some stuff while working on this, it makes a 25% performance difference.  That's not entirely insignificant.   

Writing tests for this is surprising hard.   Still need to add some cleaned up unit tests that check for duplicate file descriptor reuse as described in this issue.

I agree with @hgrecco. Though, what would be really slick (in my opinion) is an optional argument for decimal precion instead of forcing a compact representation on the user. That way, pint could return whatever  prefix of a unit is necessary to have `n` decimal places.

For example:
```
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
q = 123000e-9 * ureg('m')
q.to_compact()
q.to_compact(prec=3)

>>> 123 micrometer
>>> 0.123 millimeter
```

My thought would be to pass in a proper variance to extract (and it can be used anywhere else in the same way) as that would require the fewest changes to the code. That variance could be populated as the variance if it is provided, the square of the error if that is provided, or do the poisson + sky rms + readnoise if neither of those are provided (which is what Sextractor does internally). Then we could just use the PIXTLIST(pixt, var) where ever we need the uncertainties. I think that would preserve the behavior of SExtactor (which we should for sure test against) without having too add to the code complexity of SEP too much. What do you think?

One note here:

**Why `CALLQUEUE` don't need to put into `sandbox`?**

Because in sandbox, all inspectors will be destroyed after each test case, then `CALLQUEUE` will become empty at the same time. But for new implementation, we need to do reset `SpyCall` in sandbox since it's not directly related to inspectors anymore.

The text and the picture go in separate arguments if that's what you're asking...

`send_message()` does in fact also accept a file directly, so assuming your image has a filename (i.e. you've opened a physical file as opposed to using `BytesIO`), you can skip the call to `upload_image()` and just pass the file directly:

```python
await conv.send_message([], image_file=f)  # [] for an empty message (no segments)
```

Text-only message using [segments](https://hangups.readthedocs.io/en/latest/developer_guide/high_level.html#hangups.ChatMessageSegment):

```python
await conv.send_message([ChatMessageSegment("Hello"), ...])
```

Text and an image:

```python
await conv.send_message([ChatMessageSegment(...)], image_file=f)
```

This sort of thing works for me using master, maybe this was a bug recently fixed. I used the script below. Maybe try installing from master for now? I plan a new release quite soon

Aside 1: I recommend you use .read() and .write(), that way you don't have to know what type the data is; it will call the correct method for you.

Aside 2: you probably don't want to use the default compression tiling.

import numpy
import fitsio

data=numpy.arange(20*20,dtype='f8').reshape(20,20)

hdr0={'test':3}
hdr1={'blah':'hello'}

with fitsio.FITS('test.fits','rw',clobber=True) as fout:
    fout.write(data=None,header=hdr0)
    fout.write(data,
               header=hdr1,
               extname='FS1',
               compress='RICE')

Hello longcloud,

Aspentech and SimSci are extremely large corporations with the resources to conduct comprehensive literature surveys or simply purchase their data. For example, I believe Aspentech licenses the complete VLE database from DDBST to perform regressions to obtain their binary interaction parameters.  DDBST has [1024504 data sets](http://ddbonline.ddbst.com/DDBSearch/onlineddboverview.exe?submit=Statistics) currently, and charges on the order of $35 USD per set. The multiplication of those values results in a very large number. 

You read the line correctly; kijs are assumed 0 unless specified by the user. For example, the H2S-CH4 system is known to have kij=0.08. To use that value, use:
```
>>> SRKMIX(T=190, P=40.53E5, Tcs=[373.2, 190.564], Pcs=[8936865.0, 4599000.0], omegas=[0.1, 0.008], zs=[ 0.07, 0.93], kijs=[[0, .08], [0.08, 0]])
<thermo.eos_mix.SRKMIX object at 0x7fe0e2f33590>
```

If you are aware of a large database of free VLE data, please let me know. The ones I am aware of are small and not critically evaluated:
* https://www.cheric.org/research/kdb/hcvle/hcvle.php
* http://vle-calc.com/index.html

Sincerely,
Caleb

that strange time.  I tried various upstart options to change when the

> By the way, can you tell me why you do the first poll with timeout_ms set to 0? What does it mean for the group to be rebalanced?

A consumer group will split all processing partitions between consumers. For example when we have 2 consumers with group "my_sessions" and the subscribed topic has 10 partitions, each consumer will only fetch from 5 partitions. The process of *assignment* of those partitions I called a rebalance, but more precise would be "joining" the group. More info here: https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#intro_consumers

> I ran your script on an existing topic that I was using to test this on. When I tried to run the script using the same group name as my existing test, it exited silently. But when I changed the group name to something random, I got the exception.

That is expected, I missed the `NoOffsetForPartitionError` part. You will get that error if it's a fresh topic and no offsets has been commited yet, so it does not know where to start. As for `OffsetOutOfRangeError` is raised if we know where to read, but that data is not present on our broker.
As for your topic, that exited silently I may only presume, that you actually have data on offset `21312312331`...

What I don't understand is why it would sometimes have duplicate messages and sometimes not... I haven't looked at the code, but I'm guessing the consumer maintains an internal offset within the `KafkaConsumer` process even though it isn't ever committed to the broker. 

Perhaps something changed in the 1.3.1 to 1.3.3 code that causes the consumer to more frequently lose connection to the broker, and then when that connection comes back it re-initializes it's offsets from the broker instead of pulling from it's in-memory offset? IDK...

Looked a bit deeper. This isn't as serious as I thought (the title made me think there was message loss -- there isn't). Basically the issue here is that when using consumer.poll() with max_records, there will be "unnecessary" additional FetchRequests if/when the "max_record" cutoff lands in a compacted (i.e., deleted) message range.

I looked at the official java client and it appears that this logic is the same through 0.10.2.1:
https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/0.10.2.1/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/internals/Fetcher.java#L524

The v2 message format refactor in 0.11.0.0 updates a large part of the Fetcher, including a change to how fetch responses are buffered and iterated internally. I think it would be better to keep aligned with the java client than to attempt to tweak the logic for optimizations like this.

So I'm going to wontfix on this PR. If you want to avoid these refetches in the current kafka-python implementation, you might consider switching to the iterator interface. It does not have the same problem since it maintains internal state within a generator / yield loop:

```
max_records = 50
msgs = [next(consumer) for _ in range(max_records)]
```
If it is possible, I can help to support such interfaces.

Hi @kotoroshinoto, the short answer is that PyVCF only uses the right-most value for a given key.

I don't think the example you give is valid use of the INFO column. I had a quick look at the complete COSMIC data myself and the VCF header simply states that `GENE` and `STRAND` are singular string values:

    ##INFO=<ID=GENE,Number=1,Type=String,Description="Gene name">
    ##INFO=<ID=STRAND,Number=1,Type=String,Description="Gene strand">

Also, it seems that the two values always have the exact same value (I only checked `/files/grch38/cosmic/v75/VCF/CosmicCodingMuts.vcf.gz`). In that case you should be fine with the current behaviour of PyVCF, but I have no clue why the values are duplicated.

I'll close this issue now, since it doesn't look like a bug in PyVCF. If you have any further questions or problems feel free to re-open or comment.
Thank you!

Please ignore the 2nd half of my previous comment - I followed a link from email and thought this was the related issue #213.

Someone above mentioned that pyodbc might require a prepare for binding - it is doing that now because I originally thought ODBC required it but the SQL Server docs say otherwise.  We should be able to call SQLExecuteW and bind parameters.  I suspect many drivers will not, but we can default to this and use a flag for those that don't.

One issue to be aware of is ODBC only allows one prepared statement per HSTMT, which is what we represent with a cursor.  If you need to switch between two prepared statements you'd want to create two cursors.

I'm aware of the int / bigint, but I thought it was behaving like the 3.x version.

We don't know the target type for parameters without an execution plan compile and query, so we use `int` when the value fits ODBC's definition of int and `bigint` when bigger.  The first 4.0 used bigint exclusively but some databases don't even support it so I had to revert to the current logic.

I wouldn't expect a performance hit if the conversion is the parameter, but SQL Server often misses easy conversions like that.  A good C / C++ compiler would make a 64-temporary for comparisons in a loop - I don't know why SQL Server can't.

This could be a good argument for manually setting types, but since it requires code changes in the client would a case be just as good: "select * from t where id = (cast ? as bigint)"?
A heads-up for anyone who is working on related things later: The setup machinery will use existing egg info, so you need to delete your old egg-info directory before this fix takes effect.

Do the queries involve string parameters of widely-varying lengths? If so, perhaps the performance degradation is due to the proliferation of many execution plans in the cache, as mentioned in https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/issues/213 . I have some suggestions to try in that issue.

Another potential cause, also related to the plan cache, is that prepared statements will remain allocated and usable on the server until PyODBC's Cursor objects are either garbage-collected or the close() method is called. Ensure that you are closing the Cursors after using them in your application.

Hi,

The ``prometheus_metrics_proto`` package is built and created by this project when it installs.
This project makes use of the ``pyrobuf`` dependency to build the ``prometheus_metrics_proto`` package. ``pyrobuf`` creates a package for each ``.proto`` file. This is why the ``pyrobuf_modules="proto",`` line is in the ``setup.py`` file. It's a little weird but that's what it does. So there is no need for it to be listed in the ``requirements.txt`` file.

The ``prometheus_metrics_proto`` is used to provide an efficient binary format. Currently this pacakge is tightly coupled into the project. However, the text format is likely fine for most use cases. I am planning to eventually make the ``prometheus_metrics_proto`` package an optional extra because of these kinds of issues that people seem to encounter when trying to use ``pyrobuf`` package. However, I'm really busy at work right now so not much time at the moment to make the necessary changes.

@dbaber, In a case like that, I'd probably use two Schemas paired with an `Any()`, one that requires both `p` and `q`, and one that marks `q` as optional (listed in that order, so that validation is attempted against the stricter of the two Schemas first, with the more lenient schema available as a fallback in the event that either `p` or `q` is absent, thus invalidating the payload according to the first Schema):

```python
>>> s = v.Any(
...     v.Schema({v.Required('p'): object, v.Required('q'): object}),
...     v.Schema({v.Optional('q'): object}))
>>> s({})
{}
>>> s({'q': "I'm all alone"})
{'q': "I'm all alone"}
>>> s({'p': "There's no one here beside me"})
MultipleInvalid: required key not provided @ data['q']
>>> s({'p': "My problems have all gone", 'q': "There's no one to deride me"})
{'q': "There's no one to deride me", 'p': 'My problems have all gone'}
```

I ran into a similar case.

Below fragment (no `dataclass`) works:
```
class B:
    def __init__(self, val=1):
        self.val = val
class C (B):
    pass
c = C()
assert c.val == 1
```
Making `C` into a dataclass doesn't work immediately:
```
from dataclasses import dataclass
class B:
    def __init__(self, val=1):
        self.val = val
@dataclass # <-- decorator added
class C (B):
    pass
c = C()
assert c.val == 1
```
This fragment doesn't fail on the assertion itself, but on "'C' objects has no attribute 'val'" (because `B.__init__` isn't called).

`__post_init__` is required to fix (which I'find somewhat counter-intuitive, as adding the `@dataclass` decoration shouldn't require an additional method):
```
from dataclasses import dataclass
class B:
    def __init__(self, val=1):
        self.val = val
@dataclass
class C (B):
    def __post_init__(self): # <-- method added
        super().__init__() # <-- base __init__ explicitly called
c = C()
assert c.val == 1
```
As @JimJJewett stated, I'd expect adding a call to `super().__init__()` (without args) to a `dataclass`'s generated `__init__` would resolve this and leave fewer surprises to the user.
If args *are* required (i.e. no `=1` default in `B`'s parameter list), an `__init__` would also have been needed in the non-`dataclass` case, so the user would expect they needed to supply this somehow in the `dataclass` case as well.

> The PEP doesn't say that fields are initialized in order, but I'd like to do so, anyway.

+1 from me, this adds predictability.

I just had a similar case where I want to strip_code and remove all the images, so this is my solution:

```python
img = re.compile('File|Image|ALTERNATIVE_ALIASES:', re.I)
prs = mwparserfromhell.parser.Parser().parse(wikicode)
remove_img = [f for f in prs.ifilter_wikilinks() if img.match(str(f.title))]
for f in remove_img:
   prs.remove(f)
```

And to get ALTERNATIVE_ALIASES you need to:
* Request it by API: https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php?action=query&meta=siteinfo&siprop=namespaces|namespacealiases
* get it from php code: mediawiki/core/languages/messages/MessagesXX.php (namespaceAliases)
[IMO: all approaches are out of scope of mwparserfromhell]

So the difference is this: An OrderedSet compared to another OrderedSet cares about order, which means that comparisons against other ordered collections _also_ cares about order. So in this regard, an OrderedSet compares more like a list than a set, when using the "less than or equal" operator. (Of course, it is wrong that the `issubset` method, which is implemented by using that operator, also behaves like a list comparison.)

TLDR: I'm not entirely sure that it wouldn't _also_ be a bug to make comparisons behave more like set comparisons than list comparisons.

I don't understand. If you want to write a multiplatform script printing unicode, you don't have to import anything, it should be up to consumer to fix his Python environment by installing `win-unicode-console` and enabling it (on Windows) in his `sitecustomize`. I.e. I don't think it is a good idea to import `win-unicode-console` in a consumer script. But you can still do it in a multiplatform way – just make it conditional, testing the platform.

On the other hand, if you want to write a multiplatform Unicode-fixing package, you should import each platform-specific solution depending on which platform you currently are. Wanting five platform specific packages to do nothing on other platforms just for a possibility to import them all and then enable them all seems wrong to me.

`win-unicode-console` may provide useful behavior even on Unix, but I don't think it currently does. Its core functionality is to replace the standard streams with ones based on winapi functions `ReadConsoleW` and `WriteConsoleW` in interactive console. That makes no sense on other platforms. Output redirection may bring a problem completely different to the one this package is trying to solve.

Also, what exactly are you proposing? Should I permit importing `win-unicode-console` but make its `enable` function raise an exception on other platforms? Or should `enable` do nothing on other platforms? Note that `enable` is just a composition of enabling functions for different components. What about them? Should they raise or do nothing? What about disabling functions? Also note that imporing `win_unicode_console` currently means importing its `streams` module which means importing `ctypes.windll.kernel32`, which is an error on other platforms. I.e. the proposal means a lot of decisions and possibly a lot of platform checks spread thorough the package just to support void functionality on other platforms for a reason not still apparent to me.

I'm not saying you're not right, I just don't see it. So let's continue with the discussion.

This is exactly what happens. We worked around it with by overriding the `once` method as follows:

```python
    def once(self):
        """
        Performs the operation of reading from the underlying
        connection in order to feed the stream of bytes.
        We start with a small size of two bytes to be read
        from the connection so that we can quickly parse an
        incoming frame header. Then the stream indicates
        whatever size must be read from the connection since
        it knows the frame payload length.
        It returns `False` if an error occurred at the
        socket level or during the bytes processing. Otherwise,
        it returns `True`.
        """
        if self.terminated:
            return False

        try:
            b = self.sock.recv(self.reading_buffer_size)
        except (socket.error, OSError, pyOpenSSLError) as e:
            self.unhandled_error(e)
            return False
        else:
            if not self.process(b):
                return False

        return True
```

That fix appears to be good enough for the threaded client.

```
    if (type(body) is str):

```

Say you're using spark/crunch/hive/etc.. You want to write to file `my/file`. Typical strategy is to write to `my/file-tmp-luigi-123456`. But then comes the question: How do you move/rename the file to `my/file`?. There are two wrong ways and one right away afaik.

 * plain `mv` is wrong, you can end up with nested folders
 * mkdir+raise_if_exists followed by `mv my/file-tmg-luigi-123456/* my/file/` is wrong because files are moved one by one and not atomically
 * You need a rename-dont-move file operation primitive. You can not build it using existing primitives. But there is a default implementation that's pretty close though as I've described in this PR.

I have a question on the implications of deprecating the losses_by_tag output. Consider for instance the case of two assets a1 and a2 of the same taxonomy, in a scenario risk calculation with 100 events (`number_of_ground_motion_fields=100`). Right now the algorithm used to aggregate by taxonomy is the following

1. compute the sum of the losses a1 + a2 100 times, one per each event
2. sum the 100 sums and store the output losses_by_tag-rlzs

If we deprecate `losses_by_tag-rlzs` the aggregation must be performed starting from the output `losses_by_asset` which contains the mean losses per each asset (mean means summing all the events and dividing by 100, the number of events). The algorithm would be simply summing the losses_by_asset per asset a1 with the losses_by_asset per asset a2. This will produce a number 100 times smaller than before because we were not dividing by 100 in step 1. Is this right? Should I multiply by 100 or it is the previous algorithm which was wrong?

I think you could consider creating a new gitdb issue if you have clear evidence on where the memory leak is created - usually this is hard to do, and more like chasing phantoms, especially in a GC language like python. Additionally GitDB/GitPython is in maintenance mode, and I am not investing any time in it unless it is possible to make fixes in 30m or less, which seems unlikely to be possible in this case.

A common fix for issues related with resources (like memory, filehandles) is to use the `multiprocessing` package to offload gitpython related work into its own process. That way, resources will definitely be freed. It's probably not worth doing that on a per-call basis, but one could keep these workers around for some time-period, and restart them in a certain interval.

Another solution could be to use the python bindings of libgit2 directly - it's somewhat lower-level, but depending on what you do it might still be feasible to make that change.

Hi @fredrikaverpil 

> Running `pipenv update --outdated` does not return anything while `pipenv run pip list --outdated` shows outdated dependencies.

`pip list --outdated` scans each package installed in your environment and finds if it has newer versions, while `pipenv update --outdated` takes packages specifies in `Pipfile` and **resolves** to `Pipfile.lock` with latest versions **available** and **eligible**. It means the version of a sub-dependency should follow the constraints defined by parent packages.

Haha it is actually super nuanced and really annoying from a user perspective and until I was maintaining pipenv the difference want actually that clear to me either. It’s not just you. Pretty much everyone struggles with this. 

`setup.py` is for installing an application — what do I need to use this thing if I just want to flip the switch and hit go. `Pipfile` and `requirements.txt` are for installing development dependencies— what do I need to do dev work on this app. Of course you can blur the lines and lots of people do, but here’s why it matters:

If you put installation requirements in your `setup.py`, you can actually put your _application itself_ as a dependency in your Pipfile (`pipenv install -e .` would put it in editable mode which means your pipenv environment tracks updates you make to the codebase). This means that your application is the root dependency for the resolver, and conflict resolution becomes more consistent. Then you can put any development requirements in your `dev-packages` section (like pytest). Then maybe you need deployment specific packages that aren’t really project specific, you could include those in `packages` too. Then your resulting lockfile would install your project and it’s deployment dependencies when used in production, but keep those separate from the simple installation dependencies.

Hi. Sorry to intervein again.

Idempotence is not guaranteed by the Pipfile. It is not its role.
It is the lock file that guarantee the reproductibility of the built.

Nothing prevent having a Pipfile saying this package should run on python >=2.7 and having the lock freeze the exact interpreter version, exactly like the Pipfile might not freeze the version dependencies (for ex: request=« * »), but the lock file does.

It is the responsibility of the developer to maintain its own package. Ultimately the source of truth is the lock file. The Pipfile is just a convenient way of maintaining this lock file. And today specifying the compatibility range of my package against the interpreter version is not possible within the Pipfile, while it should be so simple to use the same syntax that Pipfile support for defining dependency compatibility range.

So for myself I carry a Makefile that does this but I think this makes sense to have it handled nicely by pipenv.

This discussion is weird where several people would like to see an option available and the pipenv maintainers responding negatively. At least a « we think it is not worth it, but feel free to open a pull request ».

Regarding 

> Parsing and editing Pipfile on the other hand is significantly more complicated that requirements.txt. IDEs will most likely not support Pipfile editing for a long time.

I don't agree that requirements.txt is simpler, when trying to manually edit requirements.txt I often find myself having to lookup the syntax to get it right. This is why I think a Python executable file is a better idea. In a Python executable file the syntax doesn't need to be learnt aside from the API. The benefits of having a language level API is that it can be loaded into document browser such as [Dash](https://kapeli.com/dash) and configured IDEs will autocomplete and let the user browse the docs directly in their editor. A well designed Python API is simpler and more discoverable than a esoteric YAML/TOML/JSON based "syntax".

> This makes me wonder if setuptools should bundle its build dependencies. Rather than vendor its dependencies in general, it could bundle its build dependencies.

Can you elaborate on the distinction between the two options?

> But what you're suggesting is that the build system should suppress the declarations made by those projects, creating new, divergent wheels that omit those requirements. That sounds dangerous to me, because it creates new forms of a package that violate their source, creating issues that might be confusing and difficult to track down, but also because it won't work with existing wheels (it requires a new build). Rather than building different versions of the wheels with altered dependencies, I recommend instead having a feature in the installer (or post-installer) that strips or relaxes the constraints.

Whether I modify the source by hand, or whether the build tool interprets it differently because of a certain flag, does that really matter? In the end your wheel *is* going to be different, anyway. Consider e.g. numpy, which can be build against different BLAS. Which wheel is the "true" wheel?

Disabling of the JSON-style checking for single quoted scalars is a consequence of the following two statements:
a) 5.7. Note that escape sequences are only interpreted in double-quoted scalars
b) 5.1. To ensure readability, non-printable characters should be escaped on output, even inside such scalars.
If there is an non-printable character inside double-quoted scalar, then after parsing and exporting it should be replaced by escape sequence because of b). But it is impossible because of a). So the only way to resolve this problem is to assume that non-printable characters are allowed only inside double-quoted scalars, but not single-quoted ones.

> I tried to stick close to the Ruby implementation of stripe-mock tests. One of the difficulties is that Python has no webmock equivalent (or at least I couldn't find anything that really suited our needs).
>
> I implemented a (very) poor man's webmock by using the mock library to patch APIRequestor.request. If a stubbed request was registered for the method and URL, then the stubbed response is returned, otherwise the original APIRequestor.request is called and the request goes through to stripe-mock. We can use the patch object to assert that request was called with the expected parameters. I've also added an assert_requested convenience method that lets us assert on some of the method arguments only.

Yeah, I'm not sure I could give you a strong opinion on what the right course of action for Python is, but your implementations seems to be pretty solid.

As one more data point, did you look at Go's stripe-mock implementation at all? Like in Python it seems, mocking HTTP in Go is just not really a thing so it performs real HTTP requests against the local HTTP daemon. I guess the major disadvantage to this is that you lose your ability to put something like `assertRequested` in play, which admittedly is a fairly sizable downside.

I have to agree with @jni, the most expected behavior here would be to invert the array. As a user I would find any other approach extremely unintuitive. Inverting the array for display is fine for inspection, but when users assume the white areas are `True` we'll get into trouble fast as they do follow-up computations.

My favored approach would be to catch this case, invert the input, and warn the user about what is going on. Something like

```
"A binary TIFF image was loaded with an inverted colormap.
The values in this image were inverted on load, so its appearance matches
the expected conventions (white is True, black is False)."
```

This of course assumes we can read this metadata and catch the case.

the appveyor builds failure seems to be related to the fact that PowerShell sometimes reports a `NativeCommandError` when a process writes to STDERR (in our case, it seems to be the unittest module)

E.g. see http://stackoverflow.com/a/22615314

some users suggest to redirect stderr to null as a workaround

```diff
diff --git a/appveyor.yml b/appveyor.yml
index bcabe41..268142b 100644
--- a/appveyor.yml
+++ b/appveyor.yml
@@ -88,7 +88,7 @@ test_script:
       if ($env:BUILD_SYSTEM -eq "CMake") {
         ctest --output-on-failure --interactive-debug-mode 0 -C Debug
       } else {
-        python setup.py build test
+        python setup.py build test 2> $null
       }

 after_test:
```

Note that the custom `TestCommand` in setup.py was meant to emulate with python the  compatibility/roundtrip shell scripts written for the `bro` command line tool.
There are better ways to discover Python test modules, e.g. with setuptools [`test_suite`](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html?highlight=test_suite#test-build-package-and-run-a-unittest-suite) keyword, or with `pytest` (which also supports discovering `unittest`-style modules).

Hmm, it's not so obvious how to do this...  This issues are:

* distance.py includes a routine for the great circle distance and this
  needs to remain regardless of the choice of how to do geodesics.

* The present names for the ellipsoid distance are VincentyDistance and
  vincenty.  But these are inappropriate names for the more accurate
  method.

Here is a possible way of dealing with the situation.

* If HAVE_GEOGRAPHICLIB is true, then the code works as my pull request.

* If it is false, then GeodesicDistance and geodesic are set to vincenty
  (and the VincentyDistance and vincenty names are retained).

* In either case, the names VincentyDistance and vincenty are marked as
  deprecated.  People should be encouraged to use the name
  GeodesicDistance.

* After some suitable interval, the either/or implementation can be
  substituted (i.e., if HAVE_GEOGRAPHICLIB, then the vincenty method
  won't even be defined; otherwise vincenty method will be defined but
  will be called geodesic and not vincenty).

  --Charles

No, it never really got used.  I think Gary's idea about this was that you might want to have an object modeled as a weighted sum of several different images, and then reweight them on the fly, say to derive best fit parameters of a decomposition for instance.  @gbernstein please correct me if I'm wrong here.

But we never really used it that way, and honestly, I think that kind of functionality (e.g. #628) is better implemented in the python layer by making sums of profiles (not all of which need to be InterpolatedImages).  Our code for rendering sums with arbitrary scalings is I think just as efficient as what you would get by using the MultiplImageHelper, and more flexible as well.

So my inclination would be to just remove the MultipleImageHelper class and only have SBInterpolatedImage be constructible from a single image.  

Then for the InterpolatedKImage, I was thinking we could add a boolean argument `is_k_image` that would basically swap the roles of XTable and KTable in the constructor.  I'm sure there are some other tweaks that would be required as well, but that was my thought for the starting point.

Some initial notes on this. so we get some warnings like this

```
2018-03-15 16:08:55,422: [121226] m.ethereum:WARNING: Integer overflow at MUL instruction
2018-03-15 16:08:55,592: [121226] m.ethereum:WARNING: Integer overflow at ADD instruction 6f
2018-03-15 16:08:55,792: [121226] m.ethereum:WARNING: Integer overflow at ADD instruction 75
2018-03-15 16:08:55,978: [121226] m.ethereum:WARNING: Integer overflow at MUL instruction

```

these warnings occur in code that is emitted by solc related to the `address[]` parameter. solc emits code that parses the data according to the ABI, computes the size of the passed array, and then does a calldatacopy to copy it into memory. the overflow warnings appear in the code for computing the size of the array.

here's some output from rattle thats a bit easier to read. from an initial look, these are not false positives, but i haven't spent enough time to totally confirm

```
  <SSA::BasicBlock ofs:0x51 insns:[
    %2 = CALLDATALOAD(#0x4),
    %3 = ADD(#0x20, #0x4),
    %4 = ADD(#0x4, %2), // %4 is head element; offset
    %5 = CALLDATALOAD(%4), // %5 is nelements
    %6 = ADD(#0x20, %4), // can overflow.
    %7 = MUL(#0x20, %5),
    %8 = ADD(#0x20, %7),
    %9 = MLOAD(#0x40),// 9 is the free ptr
    %a = ADD(%9, %8), //a == 9
    MSTORE(#0x40, %a),
    MSTORE(%9, %5),
    %b = ADD(#0x20, %9),
    %c = MUL(#0x20, %5), // nelements * 32 = total bytes for args
    CALLDATACOPY(%b, %6, %c),

```
This should now be totally fixed.

To make the "foreign subscription" case work, we would need to do the following:

- Have `Subscription` instances hold direct references to their `client`.
- When listing subscriptions for a topic (as opposed to for the client/project), be able to construct new `Client` instance(s) for those from foreign projects.

The last commit divides things into 3 test categories:

**Unit**: Existing unit tests that run with `python setup.py test`

**Integration**: End-to-end tests running a few batches and decoding with configurations that cover many command line options.  These also run with `python setup.py test` and everything takes about 5 seconds on a modern laptop.

**System**: End-to-end tests where we expect perplexity and BLEU score to be near 1.  This currently includes sequence copying and sequence sorting with a small vanilla model.  These run as part of the pre-commit script and Travis CI.  They take about 5 minutes on a laptop and dramatically longer on Travis (1-2 hours?).  We can either trim them down or decide that's fine since developers can run 100% of the tests locally and we don't expect Travis to fail.

So to solve this problem backwards, one can also define a CodeCommit resource with a trigger that references the function's ARN (in the same template), and keep the function without an Event Source (which is _not_ required).

That being said, it may still be good to add support for CodeCommit _Event Source_, especially for cases where people have an existing repository (created outside of the context of that template), and then want to link this function to that repo using the well-known and persistent repo URL. Function's ARN may change, but that would not affect this link as long as repo's URL stays the same.

You could almost achieve this same thing with:

    LAYER_PATH= INTERFACE_PATH= charm build

But not quite, b/c JUJU_REPOSITORY is also used as a search location for local layers, and if that's not set, it falls back to searching the cwd. You could set JUJU_REPOSITORY=/dev/null also to make sure you get no local layers, but now we're just getting weird, and potentially stomping on other parts of the program (such as those that determine where to put the built charm).

In the end, maybe the flag is the best way? I dunno, just my $.02

I believe the libraries in question fall back to pure python implementations if they can't compile.

TBH, a lot of charms are platform specific because they use apt-get.  I don't think there's been a real push for making charms that work on non-Ubuntu systems as well as Ubuntu.  Maybe we can start addressing this with layered charms.

The other thing I'm not sure what to do with is how to actually schedule these things even if we have a good PLL kind of thing going. Should we use cron? There's probably also a way to do this with celery, which lets you say, "Run this task in 20 minutes."

But questions:

I sense there's probably a better way to do all this.

I know this issue was closed quite some time ago, but I have recently migrated 8 separate non-production (staging) Drupal sites to Deis, so I wanted to assure the OP that this _can_ be done.

I feel obligated to say that I have no love whatsoever for either Drupal or WordPress.  From an ops perspective, they were simply _not_ designed to scale horizontally, and that makes them painful to deal with.  The biggest problem I have encountered is that developers (working locally with Drupal, WordPress or other) easily lose site of the fact that production is a _distributed_ environment and depending on local disk for the storage of user-uploaded files (e.g. images) and other similar concerns is tantamount to a cardinal sin in a distributed environment.  And that's the key-- Drupal and WordPress aren't just ill-fitted for containerization, Docker, Deis, etc.  They are ill-fitted for _any_ distributed environment... unless you know what you're doing and hack on your sites quite a bit to make it tenable-- which is what we did with our 8 sites long before Deis was on the scene, simply so that we could scale them horizontally in AWS.

To make this tenable-- we first got all developers working on Drupal sites to understand that production is distributed and educated them on a few caveats that are implied by that.  First: Modules must not be installed via Drupal's admin panel.  In a distributed environment, the server that _serves_ the request to install a module will _have_ that module, but all other servers will not!  We required that any modules they wished to deploy were linked into each site's repository as a git submodule.  Then they can enable and administer the modules as usual using Drupal's admin panel.  Second, user uploaded content shouldn't be stored locally in a "files" directory.  This is harder, but there _are_ modules and libraries available that will allow you to swap local disks for S3 for _all_ user-uploaded content.  It takes some effort to set them up, from what I understand, and performance-wise, it's a bit slower.  The slowness is mainly observable when an administrator or content author browses files (for instance, to select one to embed in a page), because they are actually browsing a bucket, which may have a lot of content in it.  In practice, it seems some of the pain can be taken out of this be making sure you are using the most recent versions of AWS libraries for Drupal, since many performance issues have been tackled in there over time, and impose a sensible "directory" structure on your S3 buckets so that when you browse the bucket via some Drupal module, it's not requesting metadata for hundreds or thousands of files.

Having taken all these steps quite some time ago, these sites moved to Deis with no trouble whatsoever.  So... it's possible.

The [race detector](https://golang.org/doc/articles/race_detector.html) should definitely be on for `go test`, but it looks like Deis defines `GOTEST = $(GO) test --cover --race -v` in includes.mk and then only uses it in logger tests. So the `-race` flag isn't actually on for most of our tests.

This PR LGTM as-is, but either we should add to it or open another one to try to get all Deis tests using the `-race` flag.

I spent a few hours debugging why timeseries with very little variability take so long.
The example timeseries below (all 15.0, except the last datapoint) is taking ~578 seconds
to extract features from, where as mostly all other timeseries of the same length
are taking > 7 seconds.

```
timestamps = []
values = []
count = 0
ts = 1482644940
while count < 10075:
    count += 1
    timestamps.append(ts)
    values.append(15.0)
    ts += 60

timeseries = map(list, zip('t' * 10075, timestamps, values))
timeseries[-1][2] = 16.0

df = pd.DataFrame(timeseries, columns=['metric', 'timestamp', 'value'])
df_features = extract_features(df, column_id='metric', column_sort='timestamp', column_kind=None, column_value=None)
```

Using the new ReasonableFeatureExtractionSettings solved these edge cases, ~1.78 seconds with the loss of 6 features, so 210 features instead of 216.

```
tsf_settings = ReasonableFeatureExtractionSettings()
# Disable tqdm progress bar
tsf_settings.disable_progressbar = True
df_features = extract_features(
    df, column_id='metric', column_sort='timestamp', column_kind=None,
    column_value=None, feature_extraction_settings=tsf_settings)
```

I am very happy that @MaxBenChrist added ReasonableFeatureExtractionSettings and
I would say that it would probably be useful as default as it is going to catch
a lot of edge cases, like this type of timeseries described above.

It suggests that one or more of the tsfresh algorithms are calculating using
a sequence which is being appended for every same value in sequence and then
calculating the result and repeating the same operation over and over.  In most
timeseries this/these algorithms would probably break out of the sequence and
reset as soon as the next value was different from the last?

That's the base protocol (P2P) handshake. The sub-protocol (LES, ETH, etc) handshake must include the genesis hash, which is used (together with the network ID) to decide if we want to connect to a peer or not. Also, I believe other peers won't connect to us if we send a blank genesis hash in the handshake.

I guess my point is we must assume the genesis header for our chain is present in the DB when we start, and should never try to download it. It looks like currently that check is not explicit, but I believe it should be

I find it quite comfortable to use a diff tool of my choice, i.e.,
```bash
$diffTool <(conda list -n env1) <(conda list -n env2)
```
or for environment files
```bash
$diffTool env1.yml <(conda list -en env2)
```
with `$diffTool` being any of `diff`, `diff -y`, `sdiff`, `meld`, ...
That way use can even use a tool to do 3-way compares, like
```bash
meld base.yml <(conda list -en env1) <(conda list -en env2)
```
IMHO, a `conda compare` (if needed at all) could just be simple wrapper around a diff tool (e.g., given by a parameter `--diff-tool=sdiff`). So the only own functionality would just have to be parsing its arguments to determine whether they are YAML files or environment names/paths.

I'm fine with extensions once there is a generic extensible syntax (see [this discussion](http://talk.commonmark.org/t/generic-directives-plugins-syntax/444)). What I want to avoid is doing ad-hoc extensions where someone picks some symbols and decides to make them mean a special thing, without regard for compatibility or conflicts.

I know we already make some extensions to the markdown the notebook supports. That does not mean it's OK to add more. Those extensions cause enough headaches, but so many notebooks already rely on them that we can't practically get rid of them.

I'm deliberately being hard on this, because I want to see a generic extension point in CommonMark, and that's not going to happen if we carry on doing ad-hoc extensions.

The vertical visible area of a notebook is only about half (between half and 2/3) of screen on her moderate mac book (the top bar, the browser bars, the tab bar, the scroll bar, then the Jupyter banner)

So usually a cell and the figure for that cell occupies more than the full available screen estate, hiding all context information for locating the cell in the full document.

Thus it is crucial to have some navigate aids, be it folding or a live TOC. TOC may be more efficient because it makes use of the extra horizontal space on wide monitors, without cluttering the existing notebook cells.

I tend to agree with @minrk that this is used infrequently and should probably be in the View menu. At the same time it doesn't take up much space in the toolbar.

But, in either case, it should be included in the View menu (even if in the toolbar as well)

I'm in the process of fixing a few things with the docs, and I noticed the "latest" version on Read the Docs is connected to the master branch. Given that almost nothing goes on in `master` besides a release every now and then, I think "latest" should point to the `development` branch instead.

Here's what I mean. Assume a program populates a field called `foo` via `**kwargs`. Later, we decide to include a named parameter `foo` in the `Model.__init__` of a future Schematics version. The program will then break, as the value passed for `foo` will be assigned to the "official" parameter instead of being interpreted as a field value.

For the above reason, and because there seems to be no tangible use case (we already have `raw_data` for precisely this purpose), I'm not in favor of adding this feature.

Right now we use [PyYAML](https://bitbucket.org/xi/pyyaml) to parse the `peru.yaml` file. If the file fails to parse, in general all we can do is print the error that the parser gives us and quit. (That's why you're running into trouble with tabs: YAML [doesn't allow them](http://www.yaml.org/faq.html).

That's super unhelpful! It looks like what's happening is that, by omitting the colon, you unintentionally turned a map into a string. If you had had further fields it would've been a genuine parse error, but unfortunately it's still valid YAML. So peru starts trying to treat the contents of your rule like a dictionary, and we get this `AttributeError`. We should definitely do some explicit typechecking to prevent this error. Marking as a bug. Thanks for the report.

So the `split: invalid option -- 'p'` issue is due to the fact that `pigz -p 1` is a subcommand that's contained within the `filter=` parameter of `split`. There's a problem here in that this works in docker because docker containers support running pipes within a single container easily, this allows for python subprocess calls to call pipe commands and subcommands within pipes by using the `pipes.quote` function for a particular parameter subcommand like `filter=pigz -p 1`.

It seems that Singularity containers don't support this feature (e.g. Singularity doesn't have a `--entrypoint /bin/bash [tool] -c`) and thus I had to work around this by piping between singularity calls instead of calling a piped command within a single singularity container like how we've done it in Docker. I then make my python subprocess calls by merging the command list into a single string and then passing the `shell=True` parameter into `subprocess.check_call`

I'm not entirely sure how to work around this problem. I was wondering if you have any insight into this since I have some trouble navigating string formatting for subcommands. I'll check again if I can wrap subcommands using pythons `pipes.quote` function.

> Does the machine that runs the unit tests support tens of GBs of memory?

No, but the very point of pipes is that their memory overhead is O(1) not O(n), with n being the number of bytes going through them. As we'll see there is a problem with pipes coming out of a docker container, but we'll cross that bridge when we get there.

You also don't need to pre-generate a file since that, again, contradicts the point of pipes. You can read infinite amounts of random data from `/dev/urandom`. Read up on that and `dd`, `tee` and `md5sum` or `md5`.  You will need to prove in your tests that the data going into a pipe is the same as the data coming out of it, especially if the pipe crosses a container boundary.

Here's an idea:

```
dd if=/dev/urandom bs=1m count=100 | tee >(md5 1>&2) | gzip | gunzip | md5 1>&2
```

Just noticed what is happening here. The client is expecting a list of routes, however it gets a 0 length response instead. The ``onroutes`` function gets confused with a 0 length response, and tries to parse routes from it, which fails with an error.

The confusion in ``onroutes`` occurs because of the following behaviour of the split function:

```
>>> "".split(b'\n')
['']
```

So could be two issues here:

1. Why are routes not getting returned?
2. We should not crash if no routes are getting returned.

Ok, I have resolved the issue.
For anyone with a similar problem you can do the following:
SSH into your server and edit the /etc/webmin/miniserv.conf file. Change the line ssl=1 to a 0 and save out the file.
Then type /etc/init.d/webmin restart to restart the webmin service and you should then be able to access the webmin UI using http. 
Next, from the webmin UI go to webmin configuration / ssl encryption / upload certificate tab where you can then copy and paste the three elements of your letsencrypt certificate into the fields. Click save and you are done. Log out or close the web page and log back in with HTTPS enabled
Hope that helps anyone with the same problem

I agree with one thing: "load_only"/"dump_only" feilds in Meta class IS indeed confusing. It would be much easier to specify those flags directly in a field definition:
```python
class UserSchema(m.Schema):
    id = mf.String(dump_only=True)
    email = mf.String(required=True)
    password = mf.String(load_only=True)
```
So, in my understanding the Meta fields are mostly used when you're integrating with third-party schema system (e.g. SqlAlchemy), you want to automatically import all their stuff and then customize it  further. So, I would say that this support should have never existed in the core library in the first place and let integration libraries handle all those customizations.

As we just discussed, definitely a step in the right direction, I'd very much like to see something like this. It's going to be a bit of a hassle to convert existing cfg files but a user only has to do this once (and maybe we can even cook up a script to do this when the time comes). 

For the name collisions I would just raise an error and have the user make the names unique within one version. (We can later think of other ways to make it work, e.g. allowing classes to carry "tags" around so that you can provide an additional kwarg to select the version of `mdrun` that you configured. there's still the problem that you already mentioned that the API is suddenly defined in the configuration file but on the other hand this is already true because the pre- and postfixes of Gromacs commands are fully user configurable....) 

I really like the idea of doing 
```python
import gromacs
```
and not having any gromacs tools loaded (which currently incurs a significant performance penalty because each tool is run to check it's available and to extract its doc string). When `import gromacs` is cheap it's easier to use the other functionality such as the different fileformat parsers.

Ultimately, we could also look into looking for gromacs tools in the environment (say `grompp`), parse its version string, and then use it to heuristically find a suitable configuration entry, something like
```python
gromacs.use('auto')
```

The cffi version of z3py is introduced as a self-contained package. Considering cffi also applies for CPython, it could as well be used as the main (only) FFI for z3py; The drawback is, some z3py functions exposes bare C interface to invokers with the parameters announced originally as ctypes definations, after switching to the cffi version, these invocation codes need rewriting. That's why in this pull request, I made it as a standalone package (perhaps we can call it z3pypy).

Many hot projects, like `angr`, heavily rely on z3 python wrapper as the backend, whose operations are quite cpu-dense. PyPy is encouraged to be used for such applications to save python runtime, but PyPy+ctypes is much slower than either PyPy+cffi or CPython+ctypes. So it makes much sense to provide an alternative to entirely replace ctypes with cffi in z3py module.

If the cffi version (namely z3pypy) is maintained alongside the ctypes package, the synchronization between them will definitely be painful. This could be solved by merging 2 z3.py files into 1, adding global flag and conditional invocation between ctypes and cffi methods. Not too much work, but also ugly codes and extra branches :( Considering patches to the original z3.py should be easily applied to the cffi version, perhaps maintaining both is a good idea for now.

That's correct, there is no flag that magically makes everything parallel. This is a lot more complicated than turning `and_then` into `par_and_then` (the latter only uses parallelism if there are multiple sub-goals, which is relatively rare in current applications). 

OpenMP sets the number of threads according to the system configuration, so whenever the code reaches something like

```
#pragma omp parallel for
...
```

it will use as many threads as `omp_get_num_procs()` returns. 

Note that the SAT portfolio is independent of the tactic framework and options like `sat.parallel_threads` do not have an effect on tactics like `par-or` and `par-and-then`. 

In the past, there was also a portfolio built around the SMT solver (as opposed to just SAT), but that is not available anymore (it didn't work all that well anyways) and there may still be references to that on old webpages. Simple SMT portfolios without lemma sharing are easily replaced by the `par-or` tactic however.

I agree it's arguably nicer to define a layer that takes additional
get_output kwargs like this:

class MyLayer(Layer):
    get_output_kwargs = ['some_new_kwarg']

rather than:

lasagne.layers.helper._accepted_kwargs.append('some_new_kwarg')
class MyLayer(Layer):
    pass

For the first variant, basic MRO class introspection will allow assembling
a final list of kwargs that are allowed for the derived class.  Thus, only
the base Layer class will need to have 'deterministic' in its
get_output_kwargs etc.

A minor nitpick about the second variant: '_accepted_kwargs' would be part
of a public API and therefore should not start with an underscore.

"Clearly experimenters that do cross-session linking of ROIs require some field to do this."

Yes, this is absolutely 100% critical to our use case. 

One option would be that we could build an an extension that adds identifiers to ROIs by indexing into the ROI table... but then what's the point in having a standard?

At the same time, this use case may be overkill for the broader needs of the community... hence why an "optional" field is appropriate. nullable fields are not "fundamentally at odds with a standard", as they designate where particular kinds of (meta)data should live, even if they are not relevant to all uses. 

The W3C standard for HTML designates what fields can exist in headers, not that every type of field need be present or that every field needs every attribute. ISO-8601 specifies what datestamps should look like, regardless of whether they have month or day or hour or timezone specificity. But if your date has ms precision, there is a clear way to designate it as such and for others to understand it.

Indeed, this kind of flexibility is precisely what makes it a community standard as opposed to an institutional or laboratory standard. A single lab can enforce a comprehensive specification internally... as you note, this is technically easier to do than build a community standard. But the whole point of this endeavor is a community standard.

This is as good a time as any to re-hash that I think the NWB specification goes WAY overboard in enforcing required fields that should be optional (likely to the detriment of the specification). In fact, I would welcome making ROIs optional for RoiResponseSeries, period.

I have to disagree here, the result is correct IMO.
Let me explain what's going on:
- You store a concrete value to a symbolic address
- That means as long as you don't concretize the address, the value can be written to any valid solution of the BVS
- If you load from the BVS, the loading procedure will concretize the symbolic address to a certain threshold and you'll solutions for a subset of the valid addresses.
- Those addresses CAN be the destination of your write
- So the corresponding values CAN be your concret value, however that's just the case if the address of the write would be concretized to that address

=> That means the loaded value can also be the value you would receive if the write does not happen to that address. Which in this case is unconstrained memory and you receive all those other solutions. However you also see you concret value as ONE POSSIBLE solution.

Cosigned, this is definitely a bug. It's supposed to take an OrderedDict actually, though the overhead in using that is bad enough we should probably let it take a list of tuples as well. We should also probably use a less... blunt error checking mechanism there.

> Sorry, I don't understand. According to your first post, I think PathGroup also finishes in the end.

It only finished because it errored out, because the return address was unconstrained since I used blank_state. When I use entry_state, the return address should be constrained, and path_group should finish in about the same amount of time as the Explorer. However, the path_group takes, much, much longer.

I think I may have found the issue, but I'm not sure why this isn't happening for path groups. There's some anti-debugging stuff going on at `sub_40084A`, at which point the binary can go into an infinite loop. How does the Explorer not run into this problem?
(The subtext there should be, none of us see good ways to do it.)

> so I'll do something like: pg.explore(find=0x400400)

`pg.explore(find=0x400400)` will definitely give you a path explosion on any real-world sized program. You are doing a full symbolic exploration of all feasible paths in that way.

> can I just generat a Path object in angr an after use path.step() and at each step extract all the actions?

Of course. All actions will be recorded, and if the state option `TRACK_ACTION_HISTORY` is not enabled, previous actions will be discarded at each time `step()` is called. You can simply enable `TRACK_ACTION_HISTORY` in the initial state you are using, and then access `path.history.actions` to access all actions along the path.

Hi tasptz,
It sounds very much like a known bug on Windows 8.1. You can verify by repeating your tests on Windows 10. Alternatively, check the call-stack of the thread stuck on `set_option` - on Windows 8.1 calls to `IKsControl::KsProperty` would sometimes dead-lock inside the driver. 
You can try installing [KB3075872](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/kb/3075872) and [KB2919355](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/kb/2919355), designed specifically to solve this problem. 
The only potential software work-around we came up with is calling `IKsControl::KsProperty` on a separate thread and then killing it if it is taking too long. It is complicated and we run the risk of leaking resources, so we decided not to implement this in librealsense.

I had this issue with running unit tests in Django 1.11.14 and python 2.7.13.  I found that it occurred because I have different settings files for production and test servers and only want the **test** servers to use silk.  I also only wanted to add the silk urlpatterns on test servers, which I handled by implementing an `if settings.DEBUG=True:` statement in urls.py that contained the silk urlpatterns.  I had done something similar for Django Debug Toolbar in the past.

However, it seems that running unit tests in Django sets `settings.DEBUG=False` automatically, overriding the global settings.  So my configuration resulted in the silk urlpatterns not being added when unit tests were run, and caused this error to be generated.

My problem was solved by adding the `--debug-mode` flag when running unit tests, or alternatively, by checking for some requirement other than `settings.DEBUG = True` in urls.py, like `'silk' in settings.INSTALLED_APPS`.  However, I still suggest to merge the fix in #86 or do something similar. I'm not sure getting our own OUI is the best approach, since OpenThread isn't actually manufacturing or administrating devices.  I think an explicit policy that example platforms need to supply their own OUI is probably the best option, and probably a comment in the POSIX example where it sets the OUI.

The nRF 802.15.4 driver is not designed for pseudo-reset feature. The problem is that if you reset alarm peripheral (or clock), you should also reset the 802.15.4 driver.
However, there is a problem with pseudo-reset in OpenThread. When you reset the 802.15.4 it also resets all peripherals on which it depends (alarm, clock). The problem is that the USB driver also depends on the clock. If you reset the clock, but not USB, USB works incorrectly.

I think that the best way to solve this problem is to remove pseudo-reset feature from OpenThread and handle re-enumeration of the USB device on the host. Full reset is much safer and easier to maintain.

If this solution is not feasible I suggest to edit the `third_party/NordicSemiconductor/drivers/radio/platform/clock/nrf_802154_clock_sdk.c` file to make `void nrf_802154_clock_init(void)` and `void nrf_802154_clock_deinit(void)` functions empty. This should prevent re-initialization of the clock during re-initialization of the radio driver. Platform API of the driver (clock, timer, temperature) is public API and should not change.

As far as I know there is nothing specific pyfftw is lacking - I think it's just due to the way this project evolved. We are an academic-ish site working on MRI so we had some FFTW wrappers in C that simplify some of our most common operations. Later we re-wrapped these for convenience when we started using Python. We also use an in-house C/C++ arrays that can wrap Numpy arrays, in part because it provides a similar interface to our industry partners' code. At some point we will probably give pyfftw a chance in comparison, but this isn't really the focus of our research so we have to save it for rainy days.

I think the error is the statement `Failed to build pyfftw` near the top of the log you posted.  You would need to run pip in verbose mode to see where in the build things went wrong.  You can do this via `pip install pyfftw -v`.  You could also add `--log logfile.txt` to have pip save a verbose log.

Most likely the problem is that building pyFFTW requires compilation of C (Cython) code and getting the compiler set up right can be a bit of a pain on Windows.  The easiest workaround is probably to use a pre-built wheel from here:  http://www.lfd.uci.edu/~gohlke/pythonlibs/

assuming you are on 64-bit windows you want the one labeled: `pyFFTW‑0.10.4‑cp35‑cp35m‑win_amd64.whl`

you install it after downloading via:
`pip install pyFFTW‑0.10.4‑cp35‑cp35m‑win_amd64.whl`

The cases I can find where it doesn't work properly so far appear to exclusively be because something is getting printed to **sys.stdout** or **sys.stderr** instead of **self.stdout** or because some command behaves slightly differently when outputting to a pipe/file instead of a terminal.

Lets just keep this issue around for a little while until we have more experience using the **history -t** functionality in a wider variety of circumstances.

In some cases maybe we can make small changes to make a few more things go to **self.stdout**, but in other cases it is unavoidable.  Like if somebody runs a shell command, we don't have full control over that.  Or if they write a Python script and run it with **pyscript** and they put a **print()** in there, we can't prevent that either.

Some more info on this one.

To handle metropolis we swittched our logic to check if a transaction failed to a temporary hack [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/f40fc962f9c4b78eb0667b367d4e1b426191ed9f/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L81).

Until https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/658 or https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/699 is properly implemented in all clients and we have the `status` code in the receipt we are using `debug.traceTransaction(TXHASH)['structLogs'][-1]` and check if it's not a `RETURN` or a `STOP` and from that we deduce that the transaction failed or not. If it's anything other than the 2 opcodes above it has failed.

This is a temporary workaround and has to go once we got the `status` code in the transaction receipt and start checking for that instead.

Hey @antsr thanks a lot for the PR! We also use the SIM928 voltage sources, and this driver would definitely be useful. I had a few comments, some of them I also addressed in the code.

- SIM modules are not necessary SIM928, an additional check such as a `get_idn` would solve this.
- The ability to give custom names to the voltage parameters would be useful
- The `voltagedict` arg in `set_smooth` uses the slots as keys. As we usually think of the modules in terms of their names, having the possibility to provide the parameter names would be useful.
- Once a SIM slot is stuck due to an error, a reset command is needed to unfreeze it
- A way to combine modules into a new parameter would be very useful. We often perform DC scans with these slots which we want to run as fast as possible. If we use the normal `CombinedParameter`, it will iteratively set each of the modules to the desired parameter. Instead, a combined parameter can send the commands to the different modules in a single GPIB command.

Some things I could suggest:
1.  Add start time (or "delay start for x seconds")/end time (to automatically cut the stream off at a certain time) parameters
2.  Conflate some of the duplicative protocol-dependent parameters (--hds-live-edge/--hls-live-edge, --stream-segment-timeout/--hls-segment-timeout/--hds-segment-timeout, --stream-segment-retries/--hls-segment-retried/--hds-segment-retries) into singular options to simplify (because you can only be using one protocol for one execution of the script) such as --live-edge, --segment-timeout, --segment-retries.
3.  This is a big one -- remove the requirement to tell livestreamer what protocol to use (hls/hlsvariant/hds/rtmp/httpstream).  We have a few ways to get around this by:  1.  guessing based upon the URI being requested (if it ends in m3u8 try HLS first, if that fails, try HLSVARIANT; if it ends in .f4m, try HDS etc).   2.  If the user has no idea of the protocol or the the protocol is not clear from the URI provided then we can actually walk through each protocol in order to see if it matches what we are expecting in a response from the server.....It would be a heck of a lot more user friendly this way.  Of course we still let the user force a protocol if they want.
4.  I also think that the way the CLI accepts cookie and/or http-header info could be friendlier.

I'll keep that in mind and definitely make sure there's a simple get-state-from-list-of-components mode, whether that involves get_grid we'll see. In my eyes get_grid is a way for the user to manually specify properties of the grid, and get_default_state can handle getting a default grid for components if none exists already.

*_properties actually has to be created on initialization rather than at runtime for compatibility with Sympl 0.4.0. The components that are single-column have no actual reason to require x and y horizontal dimensions - they only care about the column number. It is overly restrictive to them to require those dimensions. In fact, under how Sympl is currently written for 0.4.0, this would mean those components would not be able to operate in a single-column mode unless at least one of their inputs had an explicit "x" or "y" dimension (since it needs to be able to determine the dimension length). This can be changed, but I'll have to think about whether it should be, since this would mean for example that a model of strictly 2D inputs could be used as input for a `['*', 'mid_levels']`, `['*', 'interface_levels]` component, and I'm not sure yet whether that's desirable.

The purpose of wildcards in Sympl is to handle unknown dimension names, and this case of unknown horizontal dimension names can be handled by the `'*'` wildcard.

If you do have a general purpose solver that can work given an arbitrary irregular grid, then you would use ['*', <vertical_dimension>]. But you don't, right? The current dynamics solver requires constant-latitude and constant-longitude axes, if I remember correctly. In the future, it would be very easy to implement changes allowing an arbitrary lat-lon configuration if we have a dynamics solver that can make use of it.

```py

            json.dump(data, f, indent=4,sort_keys=True, separators=(',',' : '))
        return data

    self.load_json(self, filename):
        with open(self.path + '/{}.json'.format(self.path), encoding='utf-8', mode="r") as f:
                data = json.load(f)
        return data
```

I'm sorry I've been procrastinating so much on making a decision here! I am being indecisive because this is obscure legal stuff that probably doesn't matter too much or... does it? Well, probably not enough to actually talk to a lawyer. But then I have to decide! Etc. **But** that's still no excuse to leave you hanging.

So, as far as I understand:

* Copyright notices don't really... do that much. They don't need to be present, or accurate, for copyright to apply. But they can give you a somewhat better position in court if you do have to go to court, and they're cheap, so people generally use them: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copyright_notice#Reasons_to_include_an_optional_copyright_notice
* Once a copyright notice is added, they can never ever be removed (b/c all the standard OSS licenses require that they be kept as a condition of having a license at all). Technically this even applies to copyright statements that are flat-out inaccurate. Unless the courts decided it didn't, I guess, because of some public interest consideration or something, look I'm not an IP lawyer.
* There is a rule for what they're supposed to look like: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copyright_notice#Form_of_notice_for_visually_perceptible_copies
  * Basically: "ⓒ [year] [owner]", where "year" is the "year of first publication", which is awkward here because it was originally intended for like, books, which are generally published once and then never changed. I think for our purposes basically any content changes count as a "new publication". Maybe?

Options:

* Merge this. I have some nervousness that automatically changing the year will lead to an incorrect notice that can never be removed. I guess in fact it's sort of inevitable since all software eventually becomes unmaintained. OTOH that's a long time from now and by definition will be someone else's problem. Also, seriously Nathaniel no-one cares about these technicalities. Practically every package on PyPI has broken licensing because [setuptools doesn't provide any way to comply with a term that's present in every OSS license](https://github.com/pypa/packaging-problems/issues/41). This has literally zero practical consequences.

* Decide to just keep it 2017 forever because again, who cares, and that's certainly not wrong. This is also a very popular strategy (more because of laziness than intention).

* Put something like "Copyright Ⓒ 2017 and later, Nathaniel J. Smith and contributors", and figure that that will be accurate forever.

Yeah, ok, you're right :-). I've had some bad experiences with benchmark-driven development before, and it's going to be difficult to figure out what benchmarks are actually useful, but speed is important and data is valuable. I would strongly prefer "macrobenchmarks" whenever possible, i.e. let's serve some webpages or transfer some large files or something that at least looks like solving a real problem.

The two tools I know of for tracking benchmarks over time are [airspeed velocity](https://asv.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) (used by e.g. SciPy) and [codespeed](https://github.com/tobami/codespeed/) (used by e.g. speed.python.org). There may be others. I haven't used either seriously myself. My impression is that asv is quicker to get started with, though for serious usage then any solution will need stable infrastructure to do the actual benchmark running. (Travis is not great for this.)

Benchmarks should certainly live in the python-trio/ org. I don't know whether it's better to put them in the trio repo or make a new repo – there are technical tradeoffs because you generally want to run benchmarks across multiple versions of trio. Whatever tool we use may also have a preference.

It would be nice if this infrastructure could be somehow shared or reused across projects. Maybe we want to track the speed of trio-asyncio? Both because we care about trio-asyncio, and because it's a way to get macrobenchmarks for trio itself.
